title: Google DeepMind Pre-Training Lead: How To Get a Job at a Frontier Lab | Vlad Feinberg
guest_role: Google DeepMind pre-training area lead (Gemini Flash/Flash-Lite)

sections.title: The difference between AI research and engineering
Domains that matter for the frontier

key_takeaways: Frontier labs have 'voracious demand' for kernel development and low-level engineering, and much frontier work is classical backend and distributed-systems engineering at extreme scale — DeepMind's distillation infrastructure went through 3-4 generational rewrites, each starting with a design doc, without which Flash 3.0 would not exist.

The research/applied divide is blurry: product verticals like Gemini-for-Search do hard research (factuality, grounding, source quality), while pure research teams also act as SREs for training runs; everyone must be fluid across the spectrum.

Engineering projects are a roughly deterministic DAG of milestones; research is a stochastic (even hidden) MDP — per Jacob Steinhardt's 'research as an MDP' framing — and 'research taste' is the learned intuition for estimating success probability and time cost of unproven approaches.

LLM pre-training's name of the game is scaling laws as recipes: functions from FLOPs budget to training routine, coupled with prediction rules for final loss, because each pre-training run is one-shot at unprecedented scale; the Kaplan and Chinchilla papers are table stakes for candidates.

High-leverage domains to study for lab jobs: programming-language abstractions for kernels (ThunderKittens, CuTe DSL), reinforcement learning literature (PPO/RLHF is now firmly in production), and the distributed-systems/optimization overlap (asynchrony, gradient staleness, pipelining effects on convergence).

Best signal when marketing yourself: public evidence you built something useful, e.g. contributions to open-source serving stacks like vLLM, SGLang, or TensorRT/Dynamo; for internal transfers, becoming the person who integrates LLMs effectively in your product org makes you the research team's natural partner.

His concrete hiring offer: do The Scaling Book exercises handwritten on video plus the transformer exercise from his post and he will interview you for his New York team; the work is also strong interview prep for any lab, and he'll refer strong submissions elsewhere.

Against AI-doomer 'permanent underclass' FUD: accountability keeps humans essential — 'you can't hand off blame to AI'; LLMs can review contracts but can't represent you in court because they can't be disbarred — and planning around stochastic AI components is the skill that will set people apart.

His Jeff Dean spot bonus came from unglamorous work — hyperparameter tuning and 'golfing the XLA compiler' for SFT on the first Bard release using old TPUs — after manager Rohan Anil pushed him off maximizing first-author papers; joining the important-but-menial work snowballed into leading pre-training.

Flash 2.0 war story: MoE at Flash latency seemed infeasible because sharding experts across chips explodes per-layer communication; his report Geng Yan's pipeline-prefill idea (parallelize layers instead of experts so experts stay machine-resident) hid the communication and enabled Gemini 2.0's MoE architecture, followed by 40 days of sleepless dual-shift training babysitting by a roughly five-person rotation, culminating in Flash 2.0 Thinking topping the LMSYS leaderboard just as DeepSeek-V3 launched.

Advice to his younger self: chase problems people actually face today with humility, even the menial parts of important projects, and be the kind of coworker other people want to see succeed — the amicable approach beats cynical workplace game theory for landing large multi-person projects.

notable_quotes.speaker: Vlad Feinberg
notable_quotes.quote: Until I know the sum total of humanity's bleeding edge in this topic, I'm definitely not going to be able to further that bleeding edge.

notable_quotes.speaker: Vlad Feinberg
notable_quotes.quote: You can't hand off blame to AI... they could review your contract for you or something like that, but they can't represent you in court because they can't be disbarred.

notable_quotes.speaker: Vlad Feinberg
notable_quotes.quote: We all have agency over our future and we can start investing in skills that matter for tomorrow, today.

notable_quotes.speaker: Vlad Feinberg
notable_quotes.quote: Be the kind of coworker that people would want to see succeed.
